hartley-bay-microgrid.summary: Hartley Bay's documented smart-microgrid project was a diesel-efficiency and demand-response retrofit, not a commissioned hydro hybrid. Natural Resources Canada reports a 1.05 MW diesel plant, building controls, smart meters, and an energy-management system that shifted or shed electric heating and ventilation loads. The community was still diesel supplied in 2025; a separate 948–950 kW Gitga'at Power hydro project is in development and is currently projected for 2028–2030 construction and completion if leadership proceeds.
hartley-bay-microgrid.status: The 2008-era smart-metering, efficiency, and demand-response project was commissioned and demonstrated, although continued operation of every legacy controller is not independently confirmed. BC Hydro reports 0.6 million litres of diesel consumption in Hartley Bay in 2025. The proposed hydro plant has not been built: permitting, studies, commercial negotiations, and financing remain in progress, with 2030 the current target completion year.

specifications.value: Three diesel generator sets: two 420 kW units and one 210 kW unit, totaling 1.05 MW

specifications.label: Existing annual electricity
specifications.value: Up to approximately 2 GWh/year in the demonstration-era documentation

specifications.value: 600 V generator bus stepped up to 25 kV for roughly 2 km of distribution, then stepped down for 120/240/208 V service

specifications.label: Demand-response capability
specifications.value: 61.3 kW of identified controllable load, about 15% of maximum community demand; demonstrations measured reductions up to approximately 35 kW

specifications.label: Diesel-dispatch savings estimate
specifications.value: Reconfigured generator dispatch was estimated to save about 77,000 litres and C$77,000 per year; a further 27,000 litres/year from demand response was a projection rather than a verified result

specifications.label: 2025 diesel consumption
specifications.value: Approximately 0.6 million litres

specifications.label: Proposed hydro
specifications.value: 948–950 kW small-storage/run-of-lake plant, not yet constructed

specifications.label: Proposed hydro impact
specifications.value: BC Hydro models approximately 0.5 million litres/year of diesel displacement; Coast Funds described a 95% reduction target. Both are forecasts, not operating results

specifications.label: Proposed hydro schedule and cost
specifications.value: Current developer projection is C$77.5 million, construction beginning in 2028, and completion in 2030, contingent on leadership approval and project progression

equipment.detail: Two 420 kW and one 210 kW generator sets; manufacturers and models are not disclosed in the cited technical report
Community-wide energy information and demand-response platform
Wireless, adjustable 30 A thermostats/load controllers installed at major facilities to control electric heating and ventilation loads
Wireless smart meters deployed to community buildings beginning in 2008
No battery energy-storage system is documented for the commissioned smart-microgrid phase

technicalDetails.detail: The project overlaid monitoring, building-efficiency measures, coordinated diesel dispatch, and demand response on an isolated diesel power system.
Baseboard heating, domestic hot water, HVAC, and ventilation loads at the school, clinic, community centre, and other buildings were candidates for short-duration control.
The technical report describes about 170 residents and 82 buildings, comprising 62 residential and 20 commercial or mixed-use buildings.
The demonstration-era cost of electricity was estimated at roughly C$0.67/kWh and more than C$500,000 per year; these are historical estimates, not current tariffs.
The latest primary planning evidence still treats Hartley Bay as a diesel community. The hydro contribution is zero until the separate Gitga'at Power project is commissioned.
A 5.7 km access road was completed in January 2024, commercial negotiations began in August 2025, and environmental, technical, and permitting work continues.

hefei-university-of-technology-microgrid.summary: Hefei University of Technology built a laboratory microgrid testbed with the University of New Brunswick to study multi-energy generation, power electronics, storage, and hierarchical control. The best primary descriptions identify 10 kW single-phase and 30 kVA three-phase PV sources, two 30 kW wind-generation simulators, a 5 kW fuel cell, battery and ultracapacitor storage, and two 15 kW conventional-generator simulators. This corrects legacy descriptions that treated all 60 kW of wind as physical turbines and attributed the project to the Chinese Academy of Sciences.
hefei-university-of-technology-microgrid.status: Historical academic research facility. The testbed was reported in 2008 and reviewed in 2013. HFUT researchers continued publishing related islanded-microgrid control work through at least 2018, but no primary source confirms that the original hardware configuration remains intact or operating in 2026.

5 kW fuel-cell generator; chemistry, manufacturer, and model are not disclosed
A 300 Ah electrochemical battery bank paired with a 1,800 F ultracapacitor bank

Resistive, capacitive, and inductive banks; AC and DC motors; and power-electronic loads

technicalDetails.detail: The platform was designed to test coordinated operation of heterogeneous distributed generators, storage, loads, and their power-electronic interfaces.
The reported 60 kW wind component is simulator capacity. It should not be described as installed wind-turbine nameplate capacity.

Later HFUT integrated-microgrid research demonstrated master-slave control and flexible transitions between grid-connected and islanded operation; this is related work and not proof of the exact original testbed configuration.

The primary testbed paper names HFUT and the University of New Brunswick. The Chinese Academy of Sciences is covered separately in the 2013 China review and should not be listed as an original HFUT-testbed partner without additional evidence.
HFUT faculty publication records show continuing work on decentralized power control for islanded AC microgrids in 2018, but they do not establish present operation of the 2008 hardware.

hempstead-port-washington-microgrid-feas.summary: The Town of North Hempstead's Port Washington project was a NY Prize Stage 1 feasibility study for five coordinated, site-level microgrids serving municipal, school, fire, water, library, cultural, and emergency facilities. The 2016 concept combined 616 kW of CHP, 310 kW of proposed PV, 150 kWh of storage, 40 kW of existing PV, and 1.712 MW of existing emergency generators. It remained a planning study: Port Washington does not appear on NYSERDA's official Stage 2 award list, and no commissioning evidence was found.
hempstead-port-washington-microgrid-feas.status: Stage 1 feasibility study completed in April 2016; not built. The project received a Stage 1 award but is absent from the official Stage 2 award roster. The conclusion that it did not advance is an inference from NYSERDA's award records and the lack of subsequent construction or operating documentation.

Used for feasibility optimization and economics; it was a study tool, not proposed operating control equipment

technicalDetails.detail: Five site-specific nodes were intended to coordinate as one resilience entity, but the study notes that the nodes might not all be physically interconnected.
Each site microgrid was conceived to isolate locally and support prioritized critical loads with CHP, PV, storage, and existing standby generators.
Landmark on Main Street, Port Washington Public Library, and two fire stations formed the central civic/emergency cluster.
Additional nodes covered school-district buildings, water-district infrastructure, fire headquarters, and the Town animal shelter.

The CHP output in the model equals roughly three-quarters of the listed annual load by arithmetic, but this is a planning-model result rather than measured generation. Solar and grid energy would supply the balance under normal conditions.
NYSERDA lists the project among Stage 1 winners but not among the eleven Stage 2 awards, supporting classification as an unbuilt feasibility concept.

huntington-microgrid-feasibility-study.summary: The Town of Huntington NY Prize concept linked Town Hall-area civic loads, Huntington Hospital, and the wastewater treatment plant through a 13.8 kV community microgrid. Its Stage 1 design proposed a 2.8 MW fuel-cell CHP system, 400 kW reciprocating-engine CHP, a 1.3 MW peaking generator, 560 kW of PV, 2 MW/2 MWh battery storage, a 100 kW flywheel, and 900 kW of existing diesel generation. Huntington won US$1 million for Stage 2 detailed engineering in 2017, but NYSERDA later ended the Stage 3 funding path; no construction or commissioning evidence was found.
huntington-microgrid-feasibility-study.status: Feasibility and detailed-engineering work completed or substantially advanced, but the community microgrid was not built. In October 2020 the Town stated that NYSERDA was no longer pursuing NY Prize and that no Stage 3 funding would be available. A 2021 federal research proposal referenced the facilities, but it is not evidence that the NY Prize design entered construction.

equipment.detail: Proposed 2.8 MW natural-gas fuel-cell system under a modeled PPA structure; model not finalized and system not installed
Proposed 2 MW / 2 MWh battery-inverter system; chemistry and vendor not finalized

Proposed 400 kW natural-gas engine-generator at the Town Hall node
Proposed 400 kW unit modeled with approximately 25% digester gas and 75% natural gas
Proposed 1.3 MW natural-gas generator plus an existing 900 kW diesel standby unit
Concept included reclosers, protective relays, RTUs, fiber communications, GPS/IRIG-B time synchronization, and a supervisory microgrid controller; final makes/models were not selected

technicalDetails.detail: The proposed ring connected the wastewater plant and hospital underground, while an overhead link reached the Town Hall area. The Town Hall cluster was designed to retain an independent island if the overhead tie failed.
Storage would bridge transitions and regulate voltage and frequency while dispatchable generators started; the fuel cell was treated as grid-following rather than the primary grid-forming source.
The control concept shed load to about 80% of available online distributed-generation capacity and restored loads according to criticality and reserve.
The feasibility design targeted roughly 70–80% state of charge before a forecast islanding event to preserve transition reserve.
Huntington moved from a US$100,000 Stage 1 study to a US$1 million Stage 2 engineering award in 2017 and was preparing for Stage 3 by 2019.

The Town reported in October 2020 that NYSERDA would not offer Stage 3 construction funding and sought FEMA BRIC funding instead. No evidence located shows that alternative funding produced the designed microgrid.

A January 2021 Town resolution supported a DOE Connected Communities proposal with Ecogy, Brookhaven National Laboratory, Stony Brook University, and Pacific Northwest National Laboratory; this was a proposal, not commissioning documentation.

huatacondo-microgrid.summary: Energía Sustentable Cóndor (ESUSCON) is the isolated community microgrid inaugurated in Huatacondo, Chile, in 2010 by the University of Chile, Compañía Minera Doña Inés de Collahuasi, and the community. It expanded electricity service from roughly 8–10 hours per day to continuous service using diesel, tracking PV, a small wind turbine, lead-acid storage, advanced dispatch, and a community-facing 'Social SCADA.' Published capacity figures conflict by date and boundary: the strongest monitored configuration reports 23 kW PV, 108 kW diesel, and 27 kW/127 kWh storage, while earlier control papers report 22 kW PV, 5 kW wind, and 120 kW diesel.
huatacondo-microgrid.status: Commissioned in 2010 and directly described as operating in a 2019 University of Chile thesis. Monitoring papers analyze 2011–2013 operation. Later publications continue to use ESUSCON as a live case study, but no primary 2026 operating report or current equipment inventory was located, so present-day status beyond the last direct confirmation is unverified.

specifications.value: 24-hour service after commissioning, compared with about 10 hours on weekdays and 8 hours on weekends under the previous diesel-only schedule

specifications.label: Observed renewable contribution
specifications.value: A January 2011 operating analysis reported a 21% renewable-to-total generation ratio and approximately 0.191 litres of diesel per generated kWh

specifications.label: Observed PV capacity factor
specifications.value: Approximately 5% for the early monitored interval, including losses and operational limitations

specifications.label: Demonstration claim

equipment.detail: Tracking PV field reported as 22–23 kW and 84 modules; manufacturer and module model are not disclosed in the cited technical sources
Small wind turbine reported as 5 kW in the 2014 control paper and 3 kW in a project summary; it is omitted from the monitored single-line capacity table
Published nameplate values range from 108 to 150 kW depending on source and year; make and model are undisclosed
Lead-acid bank reported as 27 kW / 127 kWh in monitoring literature; project accounts also describe 96 batteries and an alternative 170 kWh total
SCADA and energy-management software forecast load and renewable output and optimize diesel, battery, and controllable-load schedules
Social SCADA traffic-light indicators communicated red, yellow, and green demand conditions to residents
Water-pumping demand could be shifted within operational constraints as part of the optimized schedule

technicalDetails.detail: The robust EMS formulation used 15-minute scheduling intervals and a two-day prediction horizon for solar, wind, and load uncertainty.
Social SCADA translated system conditions into simple visual signals, allowing the neighborhood council and households to participate in load management.
Monitoring literature records failure of the PV tracking mechanism and a repair period of roughly two months, helping explain the low early PV capacity factor.

The 23/108/127 figures come from a monitored-system paper, while 22/5/120 and 22/3/150/170 appear in control and demonstration descriptions. They likely reflect different dates, rating conventions, or system boundaries and cannot be safely collapsed into one exact configuration.
A 2019 thesis reports that the original C# SCADA code was difficult to maintain and describes a modular redesign while stating that ESUSCON had operated since 2010 with Energy Center support.

Diesel remained the firm source; PV, wind, and storage reduced fuel consumption and enabled continuous service. The most defensible measured renewable share is the dated 21% early-operation value, not a current annual mix.

ilse-of-eigg-microgrid.summary: Eigg Electric is an operating, community-owned island microgrid commissioned in 2008. The current system combines about 170 kW of PV, 112 kW of hydro, 24 kW of wind, two 64 kW backup diesel generators, and a substantially upgraded Rolls/SMA battery system of roughly 714 kWh usable-to-50%-depth-of-discharge basis and 216 kW inverter capacity. Current project sources report approximately 93–95% renewable electricity, while a new 300 kW wind project remains in planning rather than operation.
ilse-of-eigg-microgrid.status: Operating. Eigg Electric's official site is current, contractor records document PV and storage upgrades through 2024–2025, and Companies House shows the operating company filing accounts through March 2025. Three proposed 100 kW wind turbines are in planning and fundraising and must not be counted in present capacity.

Four 6 kW turbines, 24 kW total
Two automatic-start 64 kW diesel generators; manufacturer and model are not identified
In-home energy monitors help customers remain within connection limits

technicalDetails.detail: SMA Sunny Island battery inverters establish island voltage and frequency and coordinate renewable generation, storage, and diesel backup.
The generators automatically start at approximately 50% battery state of charge and stop around 90%.
Frequency-based controls route excess energy to community heating loads and wind dump heaters and can throttle hydro output when batteries are full.

The original system had roughly 212 kWh usable at 50% depth of discharge and 60 kW of inverter capacity; an intermediate configuration reached about 530 kWh and 156 kW before the 2024 upgrade to roughly 714 kWh and 216 kW.

Diesel is standby firm capacity, not the dominant annual energy source. The official site says diesel generators run less than 17% of the time on average; this runtime percentage is not the same as energy share.
The 5 kW household cap limits simultaneous electric heating, transport, cooking, and conventional loads. Current planning includes a second 5 kW household circuit for electrification growth.
Eigg Electric Ltd remains active in the UK register with accounts filed through the year ending March 2025, corroborating ongoing institutional operation.

indian-head-nwc.summary: Naval Support Facility Indian Head progressed from a 2009–2010 Sandia energy-surety concept to a real 13.2 kV microgrid delivered under MILCON P-222 in 2015. The installed electrical resources are a 4.6 MW natural-gas combustion-turbine CHP plant and two 2.5 MW diesel standby generators, totaling 9.6 MW of on-site generation. A frequently cited '15 MW microgrid' label describes the served system/load class rather than generation nameplate. The Navy replaced the old coal-fired central steam plant with decentralized natural-gas steam plants and the CHP facility; in 2023 it privatized the base distribution system to SMECO while retaining the cogeneration plant.
indian-head-nwc.status: Operational. The first islanding test occurred in August 2015, and a 2018 commissioning review documented corrective work after more than two years of operation. A 2023 NAVFAC utility-privatization award requires the Navy-owned cogeneration plant to remain able to feed the distribution system, providing the latest direct evidence of continuing microgrid capability, though no recent annual islanding or energy-performance statistics were found.

specifications.value: 13.2 kV distribution and islanded operation

specifications.label: Utility service
specifications.value: Two 69/13.2 kV substation transformers on the SMECO interconnection

specifications.label: Construction schedule
specifications.value: MILCON P-222 construction ran from approximately June 2013 to October 2015; the first temporary-configuration microgrid test occurred in August 2015

specifications.label: Decentralized steam
specifications.value: Seven natural-gas nodal plants rated approximately 200–750 boiler horsepower; these are thermal assets and should not be counted as electric generation

specifications.label: Coal displacement
specifications.value: The previous central plant burned approximately 15,000 tons of coal per year before replacement

specifications.label: Early emissions result
specifications.value: For the first ten months of the new decentralized steam system, the Navy reported total air pollution down 42% (about 300 tons), particulate matter and NOx down 28%, and SO2 down 44%; these are site steam-system results, not solely microgrid-electric savings

equipment.detail: 4.6 MW natural-gas combustion turbine and heat-recovery steam generator; manufacturer and model are not identified in the cited sources
Two 2.5 MW diesel generator sets; manufacturer and model are not disclosed
SEL protective relays programmed for coordinated utility-connected and island protection; exact model numbers are not disclosed
Centralized SCADA and Utilities and Energy Management controls coordinate breakers, generators, load priorities, and islanding
Uninterruptible-power supplies were added or corrected so protective and fail-safe controls would remain functional through utility-loss events
Seven decentralized natural-gas boiler plants, approximately 200–750 hp each, plus the Strauss CHP steam output

technicalDetails.detail: The system can separate from the utility, prioritize and shed mission loads, synchronize local generators, and restore load as generation becomes available.

Sandia's 2009–2010 Energy Surety Microgrid work was a consequence-based conceptual design. The later P-222 construction documents the actual commissioned system and should be used for installed equipment.
The installed generator arithmetic is 4.6 MW plus 5 MW, or 9.6 MW. The presentation's 15 MW microgrid label is best interpreted as a system or served-load rating, not generation nameplate.

The 2018 review found false breaker-failure indications, generator-availability and fuel-control problems, and a combustion-turbine trip following utility loss; control logic, recovery sequencing, UPS coverage, and turbine isolation were corrected.
The base used overhead 13.2 kV delta distribution. Microgrid protection had to accommodate low fault current and different source configurations during island operation.

SMECO received a 50-year contract to own, operate, maintain, and modernize the electric distribution system. The Navy retained the cogeneration plant, which must continue to be capable of supplying the privatized network.
Normal electricity can come from the SMECO grid and the gas CHP plant; diesel provides standby/island support. The cited sources do not publish a current annual MWh or fuel-share breakdown.

intelligent-dc-microgrid-living-lab.summary: Aalborg University's Intelligent DC Microgrid Living Lab (iDClab) was a 2014–2017 research project comparing a multivoltage DC apartment microgrid with a conventional 230 V AC apartment under realistic residential use. The documented DC architecture included 380 V down to 12 V buses, four bidirectional TDK-Lambda EZA2500 converters, storage, programmable/real loads, meters, and proposed interfaces for PV, wind, electric vehicles, and hydrogen micro-CHP. A partnered North China Electric Power University facility used separate 750 V DC buses and must not be merged into the Aalborg equipment inventory.
intelligent-dc-microgrid-living-lab.status: The funded iDClab project is finished; Aalborg records its duration as January 2014 through December 2017. A 2020 IoT-MGLab publication and Aalborg's current Microgrids and Energy Internet Laboratory show related research continuing with newer infrastructure, but neither source proves that the original apartment testbed remains in its 2015 configuration.

organizations.name: Danish Council for Strategic Research

organizations.name: TDK-Lambda

specifications.label: Project duration
specifications.value: 1 January 2014 to 31 December 2017

specifications.label: Funding
specifications.value: DKK 5,396,587 from the Danish Council for Strategic Research

specifications.label: Danish living-lab comparison
specifications.value: Two fully functional apartment sections: one 230 V, 50 Hz AC microgrid and one multibus DC microgrid

specifications.label: DC bus levels
specifications.value: 380 V DC stepped through lower-voltage buses down to 12 V DC; project descriptions also identify 48 V and 24 V intermediate levels

specifications.label: Bidirectional conversion
specifications.value: Four TDK-Lambda EZA2500 bidirectional DC/DC converters in the documented laboratory setup

specifications.label: Chinese partner lab
specifications.value: Separate NCEPU research platform with two 750 V DC buses and a diagrammed 1 MVA power-electronic transformer

specifications.label: Current broader AAU lab resources
specifications.value: Aalborg's successor/current laboratory lists an 80 kW bidirectional supply and 45 kVA grid simulator; these are not capacities of the original iDClab apartment system

equipment.detail: Four converters used to route energy among DC buses and storage/source interfaces
Advanced multiutility metering concept covering electricity, gas, and district heat
Battery storage and associated bidirectional interfaces are part of the platform; chemistry, voltage, and kWh capacity are not disclosed in the core project paper
The platform was designed for PV and wind integration, but the paper does not establish nameplate capacity or final installed manufacturer/model for either source
Research concept combining electrolyzer, hydrogen storage, and fuel cell for electricity and heat; no installed capacity or vendor is documented
LED lighting, DC appliances, programmable constant-power loads, and conventional AC/DC household devices for side-by-side efficiency testing
ZigBee home-area communications and gateway for measurement and device coordination
Real-time digital-control modules, meters, and medium-voltage interface terminals supported converter and microgrid experiments

technicalDetails.detail: The paired apartments enabled direct comparison of AC and DC distribution efficiency, conversion losses, control, power quality, and user behavior under similar load profiles.
The NCEPU 750 V buses, 1 MVA transformer, supercapacitors, and multiple battery chemistries belong to the separate Chinese industrial lab, not the Aalborg residential apparatus.
The project paper cites efficiency-savings percentages from other DC-distribution literature but does not report a completed, measured annual energy-savings result for iDClab itself.

A 2020 paper describes an IoT-MGLab at Aalborg with 230 V AC and 48/400 V DC buses, PV, wind, and battery resources using FIWARE/NGSI context-broker infrastructure. It is related follow-on work, not a verified unchanged continuation of iDClab.

Aalborg's current Microgrids and Energy Internet Laboratory supports microgrid, converter, intelligent-electronic-device, advanced-metering, and real-time-platform research, confirming institutional continuity rather than original-project operating status.

This was a flexible laboratory, not a utility-serving plant with a stable annual generation mix. PV, wind, storage, grid exchange, and hydrogen micro-CHP were research elements, and no annual MWh split is published.
